prompts: Karst landforms form through the chemical weathering of limestone by acidic water, typically rainwater mixed with carbon dioxide, forming a weak carbonic acid. This process, known as erosion, dissolves the limestone over time, creating features such as sinkholes, disappearing streams, and caverns. Water flowing through the rock enlarges joints and cracks, developing underground drainage systems that further shape the karst landscape. Caverns are prominent features, formed as water gradually erodes the limestone, creating extensive networks of interconnected underground chambers, which can also lead to surface collapses, forming sinkholes.
